title: Larry Loses Interest

jokes.text: It sounds like Most of those Become sidekicks, Announcers, That kind of thing. They're almost Intelligent enough.
jokes.setup: Joan explains the intelligence difference between apes and monkeys
jokes.punchline: Larry implies monkeys are smart enough to be TV sidekicks/announcers
jokes.explanation: Sharp meta-joke about TV industry that's perfectly character-specific for Larry's cynical worldview. Self-deprecating without being heavy-handed.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: It sounds like Most of those Become sidekicks, Announcers, That kind of thing. They're almost Intelligent enough.

jokes.text: Joel, are you an ape Or a monkey?
jokes.setup: Larry just learned about ape vs monkey intelligence differences
jokes.punchline: Immediately applies this to insult his crew member Joel
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders cringe moment - his inability to resist an insult even in inappropriate contexts. Great character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Joel, are you an ape Or a monkey?

jokes.text: That's the clip We'll use. The koala bear Peeing on Larry Is much funnier.
jokes.setup: Discussion about which animal clip to use for anniversary show
jokes.punchline: Casual assessment that Larry being urinated on is funnier
jokes.explanation: Solid industry cynicism about what makes good TV - Larry's humiliation as entertainment commodity.

dialogue.line: That's the clip We'll use.
The koala bear Peeing on Larry Is much funnier.

jokes.text: Classic, my ass! People are tired Of seeing hosts Being pissed on. Joe franklin did that 30 years ago.
jokes.setup: Debate over using the koala clip
jokes.punchline: Artie treating 'hosts being urinated on' as an overdone TV trope
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic delivered with perfect deadpan - treating animal urination as a tired comedy format with historical precedent. Peak Artie.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Classic, my ass! People are tired Of seeing hosts Being pissed on. Joe franklin did that 30 years ago.

jokes.text: And what They want to see Is Larry Sanders getting Hit in the nuts by a monkey.
jokes.setup: Discussion of what audience wants for anniversary show
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment that audience wants to see Larry physically harmed
jokes.explanation: Solid industry commentary on audience bloodlust, though somewhat predictable in execution.

dialogue.line: And what They want to see Is Larry Sanders getting Hit in the nuts by a monkey.

jokes.text: Good thinking, Larry. I agree. We'll use The monkey as a bumper And the koala bear For the clip package.
jokes.setup: Larry just agreed to use the monkey clip and koala as bumper
jokes.punchline: Immediately reverses the decision for no reason
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders indecisiveness and need to appear in control even when making arbitrary reversals.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Good thinking, Larry. I agree. We'll use The monkey as a bumper And the koala bear For the clip package.

jokes.text: Well, let's get rid Of the fucking blue curtain. Jazz things up a little. Let's get a red curtain, Maybe with a stripe.
jokes.setup: Larry complains the show feels repetitive every night
jokes.punchline: Artie's solution is purely cosmetic - just change curtain color
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Artie's practical but surface-level problem-solving approach to Larry's deeper existential crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, let's get rid Of the fucking blue curtain. Jazz things up a little. Let's get a red curtain, Maybe with a stripe.

jokes.text: How could I forget, Joan? That was my last Intimate relationship.
jokes.setup: Joan mentions girl from 1987 show
jokes.punchline: Larry admits this child guest was his most recent intimate relationship
jokes.explanation: Dark self-deprecating humor that reveals Larry's sad personal life through inappropriate admission. Classic Sanders overshare.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: How could I forget, Joan? That was my last Intimate relationship.

jokes.text: She doesn't do this At home. She only does it With you.
jokes.setup: Ape is misbehaving with Larry
jokes.punchline: Even animals single out Larry for bad behavior
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to Larry's general effect on others, extending the pattern to animals.

dialogue.speaker: Joan Embrey
dialogue.line: She doesn't do this At home. She only does it With you.

jokes.text: Ever since I started working On this movie, I'm telling you, I feel like I've been wasting My life doing that show.
jokes.setup: Larry talking to Francine about his movie project
jokes.punchline: Dismissing his successful TV career as waste of life
jokes.explanation: Good character development showing Larry's grass-is-greener mentality and self-destructive tendencies.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Ever since I started working On this movie, I'm telling you, I feel like I've been wasting My life doing that show.

jokes.text: He's not in yet. I know he's not in yet. Hank wants to know Where Larry is. He's not in yet. Is he sick? I don't know. Where's Larry? He's not in yet.
jokes.setup: Multiple people asking about Larry's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: Beverly's increasingly frustrated repetition of same non-answer
jokes.explanation: Good escalating repetition comedy showing office dysfunction and Beverly's breaking point.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: He's not in yet. I know he's not in yet. Hank wants to know Where Larry is. He's not in yet. Is he sick? I don't know. Where's Larry? He's not in yet.

jokes.text: I suppose she prefers The cinema and the ballet.
jokes.setup: Speculation that Francine hates the show
jokes.punchline: Artie's dismissive, classist assumption about Francine's preferences
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie character moment - defensive about TV vs 'high culture' with working-class resentment.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I suppose she prefers The cinema and the ballet.

jokes.text: I'm afraid our boy Is getting pussy-whipped. Artie, ix-nay On the ussy-whipped-pay. Quite right, Hank. Inexcusable. Forgive me, ladies.
jokes.setup: Artie makes crude comment about Larry
jokes.punchline: Hank's pig Latin 'correction' is just as crude, then formal apology
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Hank's misguided attempts at propriety. The pig Latin makes it funnier, not cleaner.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'm afraid our boy Is getting pussy-whipped.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Artie, ix-nay On the ussy-whipped-pay.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Quite right, Hank. Inexcusable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Forgive me, ladies.

jokes.text: If he's not At his house, Try yoko's place.
jokes.setup: Looking for Larry who's probably with Francine
jokes.punchline: Comparing Francine to Yoko Ono as band-breaker
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural reference that perfectly captures Artie's view of Francine as show-destroyer. Economical and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If he's not At his house, Try yoko's place.

jokes.text: I'm fluish.
jokes.setup: Larry claiming to be sick when he's clearly just avoiding work
jokes.punchline: The weak, unconvincing nature of 'fluish' as excuse
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - captures Larry's bad lying and half-hearted commitment to deception.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I'm fluish.

jokes.text: I don't think those were His real fingers.
jokes.setup: Michael Jackson says he can count his plastic surgeries on two fingers
jokes.punchline: Hank suggests Jackson's fingers are also fake
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor about Jackson's appearance, well-structured with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I don't think those were His real fingers.

jokes.text: People probably Won't remember Who Oprah winfrey is. Michael jackson Is timeless, you know?
jokes.setup: Discussion of whether MJ joke is dated
jokes.punchline: Hank thinks Oprah will be forgotten but Michael Jackson is timeless
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inverted logic perfectly suited to Hank's warped perspective on celebrity culture. Great character-specific absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: People probably Won't remember Who Oprah winfrey is. Michael jackson Is timeless, you know?

jokes.text: Like those moments During the commercial When he asks if anything's Hanging from his nose?
jokes.setup: Hank claiming special quality time with Larry
jokes.punchline: The 'quality time' is Larry's paranoid grooming checks
jokes.explanation: Perfect detail revealing both Larry's vanity/paranoia and Hank's delusions about their relationship depth.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Like those moments During the commercial When he asks if anything's Hanging from his nose?

jokes.text: Remember, you can't spell Smart ass without a-s-s.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to assert authority with writers
jokes.punchline: Pseudo-clever observation that completely undermines itself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - thinks he's being clever but the observation is meaningless. Great character-specific stupidity.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Remember, you can't spell Smart ass without a-s-s.

jokes.text: The cone of silence Is coming down.
jokes.setup: Hank about to share confidential information
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic Get Smart reference for office gossip
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's tendency to overdramatize mundane situations with pop culture references.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: The cone of silence Is coming down.

jokes.text: Who isn't?
jokes.setup: Hank says Larry might be losing interest in show
jokes.punchline: Writer immediately agrees everyone is losing interest
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response with maximum economy. Two words that completely deflate Hank's concern and reveal widespread discontent.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Who isn't?

jokes.text: You're going to see A re-energized Hank Kingsley-- On, with a capital 'o' And a capital 'n.'
jokes.setup: Hank promising to save the show with his performance
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily spelling out 'ON' for emphasis
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank over-explanation of simple concept, showing his desperate need to sound important and precise.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You're going to see A re-energized Hank Kingsley-- On, with a capital 'o' And a capital 'n.'

jokes.text: It's really hard. Pardon me? Yeah, It is hard. Yeah, very. It's hard. It really works. Boy, it is hard.
jokes.setup: Suzanne demonstrating exercise equipment
jokes.punchline: Repeated innuendo about hardness with audience reactions
jokes.explanation: Low-hanging fruit innuendo comedy. Gets reaction but lacks craft or originality - exactly the kind of lazy TV humor the show satirizes.

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne Somers
dialogue.line: It's really hard.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Pardon me?

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne Somers
dialogue.line: Yeah, It is hard.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah, very.

dialogue.speaker: Suzanne Somers
dialogue.line: It's hard. It really works.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Boy, it is hard.

jokes.text: Oh, That lucky thing.
jokes.setup: Suzanne mentions leaving equipment on floor next to bed
jokes.punchline: Larry's inappropriate sexual innuendo about exercise equipment
jokes.explanation: Moderately effective cringe moment showing Larry's inappropriate host behavior, though the innuendo is fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, That lucky thing.

jokes.text: Her boobs were falling Out of her blouse. And all I did was Make lewd innuendoes About having sex With her. I'm very, very proud.
jokes.setup: Larry reflecting on interview with Suzanne Somers
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate pride in unprofessional behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character moment revealing Larry's warped sense of professional accomplishment and complete lack of self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Her boobs were falling Out of her blouse. And all I did was Make lewd innuendoes About having sex With her. I'm very, very proud.

jokes.text: Leno couldn't flirt. It would Make people sick. Yech.
jokes.setup: Complimenting Larry's flirting ability
jokes.punchline: Visceral disgust at imagining Jay Leno flirting
jokes.explanation: Sharp industry observation with good physical reaction. Perfect Artie competitiveness disguised as Larry support.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Leno couldn't flirt. It would Make people sick. Yech.

jokes.text: Recently I was dating a solid gold dancer, and she was... everything to me. / I didn't know solid gold was still on. / It isn't. She was a solid gold dancer in 1979. I'm sorry. It was '80.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to relate through his romantic history
jokes.punchline: The revelation that his 'recent' relationship was from 1980, then corrected
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy revealing Hank's pathetic romantic history and desperate need to seem relevant.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Recently I was dating a solid gold dancer, and she was... everything to me.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I didn't know solid gold was still on.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It isn't. She was a solid gold dancer in 1979. I'm sorry. It was '80.

jokes.text: I didn't know solid gold Was still on. It isn't. She was A solid gold dancer in 1979. I'm sorry. It was '80. Well, still The peak years.
jokes.setup: Hank mentions dating a Solid Gold dancer
jokes.punchline: Larry's polite agreement that 1980 were peak years for the show
jokes.explanation: Nice character interplay with Larry's polite but dismissive response to Hank's pathetic romantic history.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I didn't know solid gold Was still on.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It isn't. She was A solid gold dancer in 1979.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It was '80.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, still The peak years.

jokes.text: You trying to tell me You're pregnant, Hank?
jokes.setup: Hank says there's a new family member
jokes.punchline: Larry misinterprets as Hank being pregnant
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke with efficient delivery, though the setup is somewhat obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You trying to tell me You're pregnant, Hank?

jokes.text: Sometimes, When kenny g is on, I get a little sleepy.
jokes.setup: Larry asks if Hank gets bored with show
jokes.punchline: Hank admits only Kenny G puts him to sleep
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor with good cultural specificity and perfect understatement for Hank's character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Sometimes, When kenny g is on, I get a little sleepy.

jokes.text: Was that a curtsy? Did you just curtsy?
jokes.setup: Hank makes some physical gesture
jokes.punchline: Larry's confused reaction to Hank's inappropriate curtsy
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy moment highlighting Hank's awkward social behavior and Larry's bewilderment.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Was that a curtsy? Did you just curtsy?

jokes.text: Thumbs, Hank.
jokes.setup: Continuation of gesture correction
jokes.punchline: Larry's simple instruction for proper gesture
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective callback with perfect economy. Shows Larry's exasperated management of Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Thumbs, Hank.

jokes.text: There's no reason For arthur to hate me. He doesn't hate you. Oh, good. I just typed 'He doesn't hate you.' Yes, he does.
jokes.setup: Larry seeking reassurance about Artie
jokes.punchline: Francine's matter-of-fact reversal and typing commentary
jokes.explanation: Good escalating contradiction with meta-commentary about her typing, revealing both characters well.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: There's no reason For arthur to hate me.

dialogue.speaker: Francine
dialogue.line: He doesn't hate you.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, good.

dialogue.speaker: Francine
dialogue.line: I just typed 'He doesn't hate you.' Yes, he does.

jokes.text: Sweat becomes you.
jokes.setup: Larry returns from running
jokes.punchline: Francine's overly romantic compliment about sweat
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing romantic cliche delivered in earnest, showing Francine's slightly affected speech patterns.

dialogue.speaker: Francine
dialogue.line: Sweat becomes you.

jokes.text: Surround sound will bring this to life.
jokes.setup: Discussing his movie script
jokes.punchline: Implying his script needs technical enhancement to work
jokes.explanation: Mild self-awareness about his creative limitations.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Surround sound will bring this to life.

jokes.text: I like it rough. Well, nevertheless. Surround sound will Bring this to life.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Larry's rough movie script
jokes.punchline: Double entendre followed by Larry's tech-focused deflection
jokes.explanation: Mild innuendo humor with Larry's characteristic avoidance of intimacy through technology talk.

dialogue.speaker: Francine
dialogue.line: I like it rough.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, nevertheless. Surround sound will Bring this to life.

jokes.text: There's oxygen In the kitchen.
jokes.setup: Larry expressing anxiety about Artie
jokes.punchline: Francine's dry suggestion he needs air/space
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery with perfect economy. Captures Francine's dry wit and practical approach to Larry's neuroses.

dialogue.speaker: Francine
dialogue.line: There's oxygen In the kitchen.

jokes.text: I like the sound Of the banging And then the bell. Or do you prefer The bell and then The banging?
jokes.setup: Larry wants a typewriter instead of computer
jokes.punchline: Overanalyzing the order of typewriter sounds
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's obsessive attention to irrelevant details and nostalgia for analog processes.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I like the sound Of the banging And then the bell. Or do you prefer The bell and then The banging?

jokes.text: He's typing? What does that mean? He'll be doing His own stuff, And we're not gonna Get anything on.
jokes.setup: Larry is writing his own material
jokes.punchline: Writers immediately understand this threatens their livelihood
jokes.explanation: Good workplace dynamics humor showing writers' immediate grasp of political implications.

dialogue.line: He's typing? What does that mean?
He'll be doing His own stuff, And we're not gonna Get anything on.

jokes.text: We'll be right in. Hank, would you do me A favor and say... [Whispering]
jokes.setup: Artie about to give Hank secret instructions
jokes.punchline: Audience can't hear the whispered instruction
jokes.explanation: Basic setup for upcoming joke with Hank's coached behavior, but weak on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: We'll be right in. Hank, would you do me A favor and say... [Whispering]

jokes.text: I am very concerned About Larry's lack Of interest in the show. Not now, Hank.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to express concern about Larry
jokes.punchline: Artie's repeated dismissal of Hank's inappropriate timing
jokes.explanation: Good running gag setup showing Hank's inability to read social cues and Artie's management style.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I am very concerned About Larry's lack Of interest in the show.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Not now, Hank.

jokes.text: Excuse Hank. Hank sometimes Forgets his boundaries.
jokes.setup: Hank's inappropriate behavior in meeting
jokes.punchline: Artie's diplomatic euphemism for Hank's social incompetence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie diplomacy and understatement. 'Forgets his boundaries' is a masterful euphemism for Hank's complete lack of social awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Excuse Hank. Hank sometimes Forgets his boundaries.

jokes.text: He's tired of getting hit In the nuts by monkeys.
jokes.setup: Explaining Larry's loss of interest in show
jokes.punchline: Blunt, literal summary of show's lowbrow content
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback with perfect economy and character voice. Francine cuts through all the workplace politics with brutal honesty about the show's content.

dialogue.line: He's tired of getting hit In the nuts by monkeys.

jokes.text: That monkey gave A lot of people pleasure With that Inappropriate act.
jokes.setup: Defending the monkey clip after Francine's dismissal
jokes.punchline: Dignified defense of monkey hitting Larry in groin
jokes.explanation: Excellent Artie moment defending lowbrow TV with formal language. 'Inappropriate act' as euphemism for groin injury is perfect character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That monkey gave A lot of people pleasure With that Inappropriate act.

jokes.text: Movies, is it? Oh, movies.
jokes.setup: Learning about Larry's movie ambitions
jokes.punchline: Artie's dismissive, condescending repetition
jokes.explanation: Perfect character reaction showing Artie's disdain for movies as pretentious compared to honest TV work.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Movies, is it? Oh, movies.

jokes.text: You guys hitting on My ex-wife?
jokes.setup: Larry walks in on private meeting
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets business meeting as romantic advance
jokes.explanation: Decent misdirection showing Larry's paranoid interpretation of innocent situations.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You guys hitting on My ex-wife?

jokes.text: How do you feel About... Harrison ford As lyle? Well... I think, you know, I'm really lyle. Or gerard depardieu.
jokes.setup: Larry wants to play his own character Lyle
jokes.punchline: Executives suggesting increasingly inappropriate alternatives
jokes.explanation: Good Hollywood satire showing executives' complete disconnection from writer's vision and casting random stars.

dialogue.speaker: Executives
dialogue.line: How do you feel About... Harrison ford As lyle?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well... I think, you know, I'm really lyle. Or gerard depardieu.

jokes.text: He went insane. He was killed in a gun battle With the police. That's a little heavy For me. No, no. Actually, It's funny.
jokes.setup: Describing tragic true story they want Larry for
jokes.punchline: Executives insisting police shooting death is 'funny'
jokes.explanation: Sharp Hollywood satire about executives' tone-deafness to tragic source material. Dark humor about commercializing real tragedy.

dialogue.line: He went insane. He was killed in a gun battle With the police.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's a little heavy For me.
No, no. Actually, It's funny.

jokes.text: You wouldn't be Playing Hank.
jokes.setup: Larry asking if he'd play himself on Larry Sanders Show story
jokes.punchline: Executive's casual insult implying he'd play the sidekick
jokes.explanation: Good setup/punchline structure with perfect economy. Cuts to Larry's ego while revealing executive attitudes.

dialogue.speaker: Executive
dialogue.line: You wouldn't be Playing Hank.

jokes.text: she's a female, all right.
jokes.setup: Watching anniversary clip of ape misbehaving
jokes.punchline: Larry's inappropriate sexist comment about female ape
jokes.explanation: Weak callback relying on cheap sexist humor. Gets cringe reaction but lacks craft or originality.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: she's a female, all right.

jokes.text: Don't take this show For granted. You insult me And everyone Who works here.
jokes.setup: Larry dismissive of anniversary show quality
jokes.punchline: Artie's serious defense of their work
jokes.explanation: Strong character moment showing Artie's pride in TV work and defense of his crew. Not purely comedic but important for character development.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Don't take this show For granted. You insult me And everyone Who works here.

jokes.text: Go ahead And see how you like Spending 14-hour days In a trailer Playing gin rummy With your hairdresser.
jokes.setup: Larry considering movie career
jokes.punchline: Artie's cynical description of movie star reality
jokes.explanation: Excellent industry insight delivered with perfect cynicism. Specific detail about gin rummy makes it memorable and realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Go ahead And see how you like Spending 14-hour days In a trailer Playing gin rummy With your hairdresser.

jokes.text: You and Hank Eating pie.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about next anniversary clip
jokes.punchline: Another absurdly simple clip concept
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective continuation of the 'greatest hits' being mundane moments. Economy saves it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You and Hank Eating pie.

jokes.text: Hear that live audience Laugh? You won't get that Making movies.
jokes.setup: Audience laughing at pie-eating clip
jokes.punchline: Artie pointing out unique value of live audience response
jokes.explanation: Good meta-moment using actual audience reaction as argument for TV over movies. Smart use of show format.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Hear that live audience Laugh? You won't get that Making movies.

jokes.text: I was just wondering-- You being so funny, How come you don't do movies?
jokes.setup: Audience Q&A after show about Larry's career
jokes.punchline: Innocent question that cuts to heart of episode's theme
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode capper with brilliant ironic timing. Audience member unknowingly asks the exact question driving Larry's crisis. Excellent meta-writing.

dialogue.speaker: Audience member
dialogue.line: I was just wondering-- You being so funny, How come you don't do movies?

tmdb_overview: As the show's traditional anniversary show approaches, Larry finds it all boring and tedious, so he plays with the idea of writing a book while rumors spread about a film role.
